Per-bot resource overrides are available on every plan; the plan's

patch.description:
Update bot metadata, compute overrides, or runtime privilege mode.

Tier, resource-override, and runtime privilege changes trigger a full K8s
reconcile. Avatar changes are metadata-only. For config changes, use PATCH
/{bot_slug}/config.

Per-bot resource overrides are available on every plan (bounded by the
plan's ``resource_maximums``). Privileged runtime mode is still gated to
plans with the ``privileged_mode`` feature; plans without it receive 403
``upgrade_required``.

delete.description: Set desired_state=deleted and enqueue reconcile notification.

get.description:
Lightweight readiness signals for chat surfaces (widget/console).

Cheap, pollable signal: ``llm_configured`` lets a surface warn — before any
send — that the bot can't respond until a credential is assigned, and
``maintenance_restart_pending`` drives a subtle "restart pending" indicator
while a workload-aware maintenance restart is in flight. Visibility-gated
exactly like ``get_bot`` so restricted bots stay 404-invisible to actors
without access.
Get live runtime storage, memory, and CPU usage for a bot.

post.description:
Restart a bot runtime container by deleting its pod.

This mirrors the operator/k9s recovery action: the provisioner deletes the
current pod and the deployment recreates it. Visibility alone is not enough
for this mutating runtime action — org owners/platform admins may restart
any visible bot, and regular users must have bot use access.

patch.description:
Update bot config. Hot-reloads to the bridge if the bot is running.

If the bot is running, pushes config to the bridge first and fails if
rejected. If not running, just persists — the reconciler will apply it
when the bot starts.

post.description:
End a guided-setup bot's onboarding (the user-facing "skip setup" action).

The human equivalent of the bot-driven ``finish_onboarding`` MCP tool: flips
``onboarding_state`` pending -> complete so the chat service stops injecting
the onboarding directive, and unassigns the onboarding-only skill + tool.
Idempotent — a no-op when the bot is not pending.

Only fields the client actually includes are changed: we forward ``UNSET``
for omitted fields so an omitted field is never mistaken for an edit. The
nullable ``description`` / ``command`` / ``args`` clear when sent an
explicit ``null``.

Force a ``tools/list`` re-handshake by bumping ``config_generation``.

The reconciler (Phase 4) picks up the generation change and re-queries
the MCP server. This endpoint only nudges the generation — it doesn't
wait for the handshake to complete.

post.description:
Re-enter the reconciler queue for a FAILED MCP server.

Resets the row to ``observed_state=pending``, clears ``last_error`` and
any stuck operator lease, and bumps ``config_generation`` so the
provisioner treats it as drift-to-apply on the next sweep. Returns
``409 Conflict`` when the row is not currently FAILED — re-entering a
healthy or in-flight row would fight the reconciler's own lease and
leak confusing errors back to the operator.

post.description: Return a GitHub install URL with a fresh state UUID.

Consume a state UUID, fetch installation metadata, write the credential.

Authenticated by the state UUID (which was issued to a specific org+user).
Session cookie is optional — if present, defense-in-depth checks
`session.user_id == row.initiated_by_user_id`. If absent (cookie expired
during the GitHub detour), the state alone is sufficient.

All errors raise BotyardError subclasses so the central handler emits
RFC 9457 Problem Details with the right ``error_code`` for the frontend.

410.description: Install state is invalid, expired, or already consumed

post.summary: List currently streaming conversations visible to the caller
post.description:
Return active streaming turns visible to the caller.

Workforce boards, chat surfaces, and other aggregate views need a
cold-start snapshot of which conversations are currently streaming. The
chat message list exposes this per conversation; this bulk endpoint applies
the same lease TTL and conversation visibility rules.

With ``all_visible=True`` the caller gets every active turn it can see and
filters locally — preferred for the chat polls, which watch an unbounded
number of conversations but only ever see a small set of live streams.
Otherwise the result is narrowed to the requested ``conversation_ids``
and/or ``bot_slugs`` (OR-combined).

patch.description:
Change a conversation's access scope (publish to org / make private).

Only the conversation **owner** (its creator) may change its scope — not org
owners. Privatizing returns a conversation to its owner, so letting a
non-owner do it would just remove their own access; and force-publishing
would expose someone's private conversation. Ownerless (integration/bot/
API-key) conversations cannot be privatized, and API keys can never move a
conversation to user-scope.

get.description: Return the live status of a vault input request group (never any value).

post.description:
Store the user-supplied secret value(s) for a request group and wake the bot.

Locks the whole pending group (one-shot), re-checks conflicts at each (possibly
user-edited) key, then for every secret either creates a new bot-owned secret or
— when the secret opted into ``allow_update`` and the requesting bot is already
authorized for the existing key — replaces just that key's value, all in one
transaction; marks the group submitted; and schedules a single post-commit wake.
Submission is **all-or-nothing** and must cover exactly the secrets in the group;
the values are never logged or echoed back.

Any user with conversation access may fulfill the request for the requesting
bot. **Broadening** the secrets to other bots or all bots is a cross-bot
distribution decision — owner-gated exactly like the normal secret modal — so
``bot_ids``/``allow_all_bots`` require org-owner role. The pending request only
authorizes storing the secrets for the bot that asked.

Send a chat message; bot response arrives over Centrifugo.

``mode=steer`` injects the message into the active turn instead of queueing
it behind one (no-op if no turn is active). Default ``queue`` preserves the
prior ordering behaviour.

Wake an idle conversation with a hidden developer message.

Starts a bot turn carrying the message as system context — invisible in
the chat UI but read by the bot's model. Intended for automations
(webhooks, schedulers, polling processes) that need to nudge a bot
without keeping a session open. If the conversation already has a
running or queued turn, nothing is sent and status is 'already_active'.

Promote an already-queued message to steer.

Forwards a steer signal to the bot, which pulls the queued message out of
its ordering gate and injects it into the active turn. Whether the message
is still queued (vs. already dispatched) is decided by the bot, which
no-ops if the steer arrived too late.

``require_conversation`` enforces access and hands us the row, so we reuse
its ``bot_id`` instead of re-fetching the conversation.

Cancel an already-queued message before it runs.

Forwards a cancel signal to the bot, which pulls the queued message out of
its ordering gate and drops it so it never runs (the proxy then marks the
turn ``cancelled``). Whether the message is still queued (vs. already
dispatched) is decided by the bot, which no-ops if the cancel arrived too
late.

``require_conversation`` enforces access and hands us the row, so we reuse
its ``bot_id`` instead of re-fetching the conversation.

get.description:
Return per-meter current counts + the active plan's gates.

When the org has no subscription, returns an empty snapshot
(``meters: []``) so the FE can render its plan-required state without
a separate code path. Caps and prices come from ``plan_meter_gates``;
counts come from the same helpers ``GateEnforcer`` uses, so what the
user sees lines up with what the cap will block on.

Return per-meter quantity time-series for the requested window.

Count-meter quantities are pulled from the billing provider, filtered
by ``external_customer_id = org_id`` (set on each customer at checkout)
so two orgs that share an email can never see each other's usage.
Resource-overage series are computed from the allocation ledger so the
chart can show the included baseline alongside the overage.

Empty ``series`` is normal for orgs without a subscription, for
orgs whose customer was created before the ``external_id`` backfill
ran, or in local dev where the metering client isn't configured.

ActiveTurnsRequest.title: ActiveTurnsRequest
ActiveTurnsRequest.description:
Request body for bulk active-turn lookup.

Two modes:

- ``all_visible=True``: return every active turn the caller can see, scoped
  only by conversation visibility. No candidate list is needed — the set of
  currently-streaming conversations is small (one short-lived lease row per
  stream), so the client can fetch the whole visible set in one request and
  filter locally. This is the mode the chat polls use, and it scales
  regardless of how many conversations a client watches.
- ``all_visible=False`` (default): narrow to the given ``conversation_ids``
  and/or ``bot_slugs`` (combined with OR). Capped per request — prefer
  ``all_visible`` for unbounded candidate sets rather than chunking.

executable_path.title: Executable Path
executable_path.description: Absolute path to the Chromium-compatible browser binary OpenClaw should launch. Defaults to the wrapper ``provision.sh`` installs, which resolves the Playwright-bundled chromium in the tools component at exec time (so the path survives tools-component hot-upgrades). Set to ``None`` to fall back to OpenClaw's PATH-based discovery (Chrome/Brave/Edge/Chromium/Chrome Canary).

no_sandbox.title: No Sandbox
no_sandbox.description: Add ``--no-sandbox`` to Chromium's launch args. Required for non-root container deployments where Chromium's setuid + namespace sandboxes can't initialise. Default ``True`` because every Botyard bot pod runs non-root.

headless.title: Headless
headless.description: Force headless launch. Required for our pods, which have no ``DISPLAY`` (no X server, no Wayland compositor). When ``executable_path`` is set, OpenClaw's plugin doesn't auto-infer headless from the binary name — it must be told explicitly via this flag. Default ``True`` because every Botyard bot pod is a server environment with no display.

BrowserConfig.title: BrowserConfig
BrowserConfig.description:
OpenClaw browser plugin configuration.

The browser plugin (powering ``mcp__openclaw__browser_*`` tools)
can launch a local Chromium-based browser or attach to a remote
CDP endpoint. Botyard ships the binary in the curated tools
component (``/components/tools/<ver>/playwright-browsers/``) and
installs a wrapper script at ``/home/openclaw/bin/chromium`` via
``bot/scripts/provision.sh``. The wrapper resolves the bundled
``chromium-<rev>/chrome-linux/chrome`` at exec time so the path
survives tools-component hot-upgrades that aren't coordinated with
a bridge redeploy (the curated tools layer can ship a different
chromium revision without rerunning provision). Pointing
``executable_path`` at the wrapper bypasses OpenClaw's discovery
cascade (Chrome → Brave → Edge → Chromium → Chrome Canary on PATH)
and avoids the bundled-revision-mismatch problem (OpenClaw vendors
a specific ``playwright-core`` whose ``browsers.json`` revision
may not match the nixpkgs-shipped one — explicit ``executable_path``
skips that revision check).

``no_sandbox=True`` is the right default for our deployment: every
bot pod is non-root with no ``CAP_SYS_ADMIN``, so Chromium's
sandbox can't initialise. Without ``--no-sandbox`` the browser
fails to start with a confusing error.

CliWatchdogConfig.title: CliWatchdogConfig
CliWatchdogConfig.description:
OpenClaw CLI watchdog overrides for the ``claude-cli`` backend.

Applied to ``cliBackends.claude-cli.reliability.watchdog`` by the
serializer. Both ``fresh`` (first turn of a session) and ``resume``
(every subsequent turn) profiles can be overridden independently.

Why we ship non-empty defaults: the upstream openclaw resume defaults
(``minMs=60_000``, ``maxMs=180_000``) are too tight for real bash
workloads — a single SQL query against a large table easily produces
no stdout for >3 minutes, and the watchdog kills the CLI mid-turn.
Our default raises ``maxMs`` to 15 min (900 s) so silent-but-progressing
tool calls don't get killed, while still detecting genuinely-stuck CLIs.

no_output_timeout_ms.title: No Output Timeout Ms
no_output_timeout_ms.description: Explicit no-output timeout in ms. When set, overrides the ratio/min/max computation. Capped by OpenClaw at ``timeoutMs - 1s``.

no_output_timeout_ratio.title: No Output Timeout Ratio
no_output_timeout_ratio.description: Fraction of the total turn ``timeoutMs`` used when ``no_output_timeout_ms`` is unset. Clamped to ``[0.05, 0.95]`` by OpenClaw.

min_ms.title: Min Ms
min_ms.description: Lower bound on the resolved no-output timeout (ms).

max_ms.title: Max Ms
max_ms.description: Upper bound on the resolved no-output timeout (ms).

CliWatchdogProfile.title: CliWatchdogProfile
CliWatchdogProfile.description:
One side of OpenClaw's CLI watchdog (fresh vs. resume turns).

Mirrors OpenClaw's per-turn-type watchdog override shape, applied to the
``cliBackends.<id>.reliability.watchdog.{fresh,resume}`` block by the
serializer.

OpenClaw resolves the per-turn no-output timeout via, roughly::

    noOutputTimeoutMs ?? clamp(timeoutMs * noOutputTimeoutRatio, minMs, maxMs)

capped by ``timeoutMs - 1s``. The upstream defaults (see
``cli-watchdog-defaults`` in the openclaw dist) put the **resume** cap at
180 s — that's the watchdog that fires when a single Bash tool call runs
silently for > 3 minutes (e.g. a Snowflake ``SELECT`` against a large
table) and SIGKILLs the whole CLI session mid-turn. Raising the cap is
the platform-level fix; bots can still override per-bot.

reserve_tokens.title: Reserve Tokens
reserve_tokens.description: Token headroom reserved for reply generation and tool output after compaction. OpenClaw's built-in is 16384; a higher reserve suits tool-heavy bots and leaves room for the compaction run itself to fit under the model context window.

reserve_tokens_floor.title: Reserve Tokens Floor
reserve_tokens_floor.description: Minimum floor enforced for reserve_tokens in compaction paths (0 disables the floor guard).

mid_turn_precheck.title: Mid Turn Precheck
mid_turn_precheck.description: Run a context-pressure precheck after each tool result so a single large result triggers compaction mid-turn instead of overflowing the next model call. OpenClaw's built-in is off.

truncate_after_compaction.title: Truncate After Compaction
truncate_after_compaction.description: Rotate the active session transcript to a compacted successor after compaction so future turns load only the summary plus tail, bounding growth. OpenClaw's built-in is off.

max_active_transcript_bytes.title: Max Active Transcript Bytes
max_active_transcript_bytes.description: Trigger proactive local compaction when the active transcript reaches this byte size, independent of (unreliable) token estimates. Default is ~200k tokens at the ~40 bytes/token observed for tool-heavy bots; tune per workload.

timeout_seconds.title: Timeout Seconds
timeout_seconds.description: Maximum wall-clock seconds allowed for one local compaction operation. Botyard defaults to 10 minutes because large history summaries can legitimately exceed OpenClaw's shorter runtime default.

CompactionConfig.title: CompactionConfig
CompactionConfig.description:
Context-compaction safeguards serialized into the openclaw config.

Defaults harden bots against the overflow -> failed-compaction ->
stuck-session-write-lock trap (upstream openclaw #699 / #70334). Only
serializers whose schema exposes these keys emit them (v2026.6.1+);
older versions ignore the fields. Designed to become per-bot
configurable from the dashboard.

truncate_after_compaction.description: Rotate the active transcript to a compacted successor.

max_active_transcript_bytes.description: Byte size that triggers proactive local compaction.

CompactionPart.title: CompactionPart

A compaction part that summarizes previous conversation history.

Compaction parts contain an opaque or readable summary of prior messages,
produced by provider-specific compaction mechanisms. They must be round-tripped
back to the same provider in subsequent requests.

For Anthropic, `content` contains a readable text summary.
For OpenAI, `content` is `None` and the encrypted data is stored in `provider_details`.

identifier.title: Identifier
identifier.description:
The identifier of the file, such as a unique ID.

This identifier can be provided to the model in a message to allow it to refer to this file in a tool call argument,
and the tool can look up the file in question by iterating over the message history and finding the matching `FileUrl`.

This identifier is only automatically passed to the model when the `FileUrl` is returned by a tool.
If you're passing the `FileUrl` as a user message, it's up to you to include a separate text part with the identifier,
e.g. "This is file <identifier>:" preceding the `FileUrl`.

It's also included in inline-text delimiters for providers that require inlining text documents, so the model can
distinguish multiple files.

code.title: Code

ErrorEvent.title: ErrorEvent
ErrorEvent.description:
Transport-level error during streaming.

``code`` is optional — populated for chat-proxy-originated errors
(bridge ``chat.error`` rejections like queue-full 409, SSE relay
timeout 504, bot pod unreachable 502/503) so the UI can render a
more specific message than the bare ``message`` allows. ``None`` for
in-process errors that don't have an HTTP code (e.g. malformed-frame
validation failures).

MaintenancePolicyConfig.title: MaintenancePolicyConfig
MaintenancePolicyConfig.description:
Editable, persisted maintenance policy (per-org default).

Stored as JSONB on ``organizations.maintenance_policy`` (``None`` = no
configured policy, fall back to the hard-coded defaults). Distinct from
:class:`MaintenanceRestartPolicy`, which is the *resolved per-restart
snapshot* the engine consumes: :func:`resolve_restart_policy` turns this
config plus the restart's urgency into that snapshot.

``enabled=False`` opts the org out of workload-aware gating entirely —
regular restarts then apply immediately (today's blunt behaviour) instead
of waiting for idle/drain, while critical restarts are always immediate
regardless of this flag.

configured.title: Configured
configured.description: True if a policy is stored for this org; False means the returned policy is the hard-coded default (nothing persisted)

MaintenanceRestartForceAppliedContent.title: MaintenanceRestartForceAppliedContent
MaintenanceRestartForceAppliedContent.description:
Typed content for an operator force-applying a pending maintenance restart.

Emitted when a platform admin invokes the "apply pending maintenance restart
now" fleet action, overriding the maintenance-window gate so a deferred /
parked restart lands immediately instead of waiting for the org's window.
``immediate`` records whether the operator chose to hard-kill this tick
(interrupting any active turn) versus a graceful drain; ``previous_phase`` is
the maintenance phase the restart was parked in before the override (e.g.
``deferred``); ``applied`` is whether the pod recreate landed within the
synchronous call (a graceful override on a still-busy bot may report ``False``
and finish draining on a later reconcile tick).

MaintenanceWindow.title: MaintenanceWindow
MaintenanceWindow.description:
A recurring local-time window during which regular restarts may apply.

A window is a daily ``[start_minute, end_minute)`` range in its own
timezone, optionally restricted to certain ISO weekdays. An overnight
window (e.g. 22:00-04:00) is expressed as two entries rather than wrapping
past midnight, which keeps :func:`is_window_open` a simple containment test.

McpPodHostMode.title: McpPodHostMode
McpPodHostMode.description:
How Botyard sets the HTTP ``Host`` when connecting to an in-cluster MCP pod.

This is a fixed property of the server's HTTP framework, declared as
configuration (like ``port``) rather than discovered at runtime:

* ``natural`` — send the pod's in-cluster Service FQDN as ``Host`` (httpx's
  default). Correct for the MCP Python SDK / uvx, Node/npx, and every
  first-party ``ghcr.io/botyard-ai/*`` wrapper image. The default.
* ``pod_localhost`` — send ``Host: localhost:<port>``. Required by servers
  that enforce DNS-rebinding host allowlists (``mark3labs/mcp-go``, e.g.
  ``grafana/mcp-grafana``), which reject the Service FQDN with
  ``403 forbidden: host not allowed``. Sending it to a server that does not
  want it (Python SDK) instead breaks the handshake with ``Session
  terminated`` (the server's ``307`` echoes the forged Host back to the
  client's own localhost).

Only meaningful for ``runtime_kind=container_image``; ignored for
``managed_remote`` (the vendor host is always preserved).

MeterAggregation.title: MeterAggregation

Supported meter aggregation functions.

Mirrors the Polar SDK ``Func`` enum — keep in sync. Polar does not
support ``COUNT`` as a meter aggregation (use ``SUM`` over a constant

included.title: Included
included.description: Portion of ``quantity`` within the included baseline, when the series carries a per-bucket split (resource-overage meters). The overage portion is ``quantity - included``. Null for count meters, whose included threshold is a flat plan scalar (``OrgUsageMeter.included_units``) the client applies as a cap instead.

MeterHistorySeries.title: MeterHistorySeries
MeterHistorySeries.description:
Per-meter usage history returned by ``GET /v1/orgs/{org_id}/usage/history``.

``points`` is empty when the meter has no events for the org in the
requested window — a fresh org or a brand-new meter both render as
a flat-zero chart without any special-casing on the FE.

MeterInfo.title: MeterInfo
MeterInfo.description: A meter definition from the billing provider.

MeterKey.title: MeterKey
MeterKey.description:
Registry meter slugs — every countable unit a plan can gate on.

All meters are metered (Polar ``metered_unit`` + optional
``meter_credit`` benefit for included units). An entry here means the
slug is recognized by the codebase; an entry in
``scripts/billing/registry.py::METER_DEFINITIONS`` wires it to the
provider.

The ``*_COUNT`` meters are AVG-over-daily-snapshots counts (bots, seats,
MCP servers). The ``*_OVERAGE`` meters bill the same way — AVG over daily
snapshots — but of the day's time-average net resource-overage from the
allocation ledger (vCore for CPU, GiB for memory/storage), priced at an
integer €/unit-month rate (the "GB-Month" convention, calendar-independent).
Each meters one billable :class:`MeteredResource` line-item independently —
the included baseline is subtracted per-interval in the collector, so the
snapshot carries net overage (Polar prices the averaged units; no
meter-credit benefit). ``memory_request`` and ``gpu`` are recorded in the
ledger but not metered to the provider yet.

MeteredResource.title: MeteredResource
MeteredResource.description:
A billable resource line-item the allocation ledger meters independently.

The ledger is long: one interval row per ``(consumer, metered_resource)``.
CPU and memory split into a reserved floor (``*_request``) and a burstable
ceiling (``*_limit``) because they are priced on both dimensions; storage and
gpu are single-dimension (fully reserved). New line-items are added by
extending this enum + a one-time migration — never by widening the table.

NativeToolCallPart.title: NativeToolCallPart
NativeToolCallPart.description:
A tool call to a native tool.

For native tools with a stable cross-provider shape (currently `tool_search`), this base
class can be promoted to a typed subclass with a narrowed `args` `TypedDict`. See
[`NativeToolSearchCallPart`][pydantic_ai.messages.NativeToolSearchCallPart] for the
canonical example.

Adding a typed subclass for a future native tool (see `pydantic_ai._tool_search` for
a worked example):

1. Add a sibling `pydantic_ai/_<name>.py` module that defines the cross-provider
   `TypedDict`s, the `NativeToolCallPart` / `NativeToolReturnPart` subclasses,
   and registers their narrowers into `_NATIVE_CALL_NARROWERS` /
   `_NATIVE_RETURN_NARROWERS` keyed by `tool_kind`. Subclass overrides
   `tool_kind: Literal['<emitter>']` to match the emitting
   [`AbstractNativeTool.kind`][pydantic_ai.native_tools.AbstractNativeTool.kind],
   and shadows `args` / `content` with a narrower type.
2. Late-import the new module from this file (alongside the existing tool-search
   import) so registration runs whenever `pydantic_ai.messages` is imported.
3. Add the subclass to `ModelResponsePart`'s discriminated union and to
   `_model_response_part_discriminator` so Pydantic deserialization auto-promotes
   on `model_validate` / `model_validate_json`.

Dispatch is by `tool_kind`, not `tool_name`. This protects users whose tools happen to
share a name with one of ours from accidentally getting their parts promoted (and
failing shape validation against the typed `args`/`content`).

The `provider_details` field carries genuinely non-portable provider extras
(e.g. Anthropic's `strategy: 'bm25' | 'regex'` for tool search). Promote a field
to a typed slot in `args` / `content` only when at least two of OpenAI, Anthropic,
and Google support it (cf. [issue #3885](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/issues/3885)).

`tool_name='mcp_server:<label>'`); typed-subclass work for MCP is tracked by
[issue #3561](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/issues/3561).

NativeToolSearchCallPart-Input.description:

Responses) where the provider executes the search and emits a native result.
The local-fallback path uses
[`ToolSearchCallPart`][pydantic_ai.messages.ToolSearchCallPart] instead.

streaming / partial-args case before parsing completes; once parsed,
`args` is a [`ToolSearchArgs`][pydantic_ai.messages.ToolSearchArgs]
`TypedDict`.

PlanFeature.description: Feature gates enabled by a subscription plan.

included_units.title: Included Units
included_units.description: Units granted per billing period at no extra charge (0 = none)

hard_cap.title: Hard Cap
hard_cap.description: Absolute ceiling enforced at write time (null = no cap)

unit_price_cents.title: Unit Price Cents
unit_price_cents.description: Overage price per metered unit, in cents (null = no overage billing)

PlanMeterGateItem.title: PlanMeterGateItem
PlanMeterGateItem.description:
One gate on a plan: included units, optional hard cap, optional overage price.

Carries every gate on the plan — both count meters (bots/seats/MCP) and
resource-overage meters (cpu/memory/storage). Both bill the same way: the
average unit held over the billing period x ``unit_price_cents`` (whole-cent
€/unit-month). Consumers distinguish the two by ``meter_key`` (overage keys
are :data:`~botyard_core.models.enums.RESOURCE_OVERAGE_METER_KEYS`); overage
gates carry no ``hard_cap`` so the count-only consumers already skip them.

PlanResponse.title: PlanResponse
PlanResponse.description:
Plan response.

``meter_gates`` carries every gate on the plan — both count meters
(bots/seats/MCP) and resource-overage meters (cpu/memory/storage), priced
uniformly in integer cents per unit per month. Consumers distinguish the two
by ``meter_key`` (overage keys are ``RESOURCE_OVERAGE_METER_KEYS``); overage
gates carry no ``hard_cap``, so cap/count-only consumers already skip them.

``resource_defaults`` is the plan's per-consumer *included* resource
baseline, bucketed by consumer type (e.g. ``{"bot": {...}}``). It is the
floor billed at no charge: a consumer's metered resource overage is
``max(0, reserved - included)`` per line-item, so the UI needs this baseline
to project the overage cost of a resource change before applying it.

``resource_maximums`` is the plan's per-consumer *hard ceiling*, bucketed the
same way. It is SPARSE: each set line-item caps that dimension (the API
rejects a bot whose resolved resources exceed it with a 422); an absent
line-item — or an absent consumer, or an empty map — is uncapped. Distinct
from the overage gates (which *price* allocation above the included baseline)
— this is the absolute limit that can't be exceeded at any price, so the UI
needs it to warn (and block) before a user tries to save an over-limit value.

ResourceConsumerType.title: ResourceConsumerType
ResourceConsumerType.description:
Kind of entity that consumes (reserves) billable resources.

The allocation ledger is polymorphic over consumers: a bot today, an
org-scoped MCP server tomorrow. Stored as a native enum; adding a new
consumer kind is a one-line ``ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE`` migration.

ResourceQuantities.title: ResourceQuantities
ResourceQuantities.description:
A set of resource line-items as K8s quantities, keyed by metered resource.

Used in two roles, distinguished only by completeness:

* **Plan defaults** (``Plan.resource_defaults[consumer]``) — a complete base
  bundle: every billable ceiling (``cpu_limit`` / ``memory_limit`` /
  ``storage``) set; requests optional (burstable-derived when omitted). There
  is no global fallback — a consumer that can't resolve a complete bundle is
  an invariant break (see ``ResourceDefaultsUnavailableError``).
* **API ``resources``** (create / update) — a *sparse patch*: only the
  line-items the caller is changing ("send what you want to update"). Custom
  values are gated to the enterprise plan at the route. On the read path the
  block carries the bot's full effective resources.

At most one line-item per resource type.

resource_type.description: Which metered resource this quantity sets.

ResourceQuantity.title: ResourceQuantity
ResourceQuantity.description: One resource line-item as a K8s quantity — ``(metered resource, quantity)``.

RestartUrgency.title: RestartUrgency
RestartUrgency.description:
How aggressively a pod-recreating maintenance restart may proceed.

Used by the maintenance-restart engine to pick a policy. Hard-coded at the
trigger site for now (rollouts/spec changes are not yet tagged with an
urgency); designed to be set explicitly once rollouts carry it.

* ``REGULAR`` — honour maintenance windows and the full grace wait.
* ``CRITICAL`` — apply as soon as possible: bypass maintenance windows and
  use a minimal grace (preserves today's immediate-apply behaviour so
  urgent security/runtime rollouts do not regress).

RetryPromptPart-Input.title: RetryPromptPart
RetryPromptPart-Input.description:
A message back to a model asking it to try again.

This can be sent for a number of reasons:

* Pydantic validation of tool arguments failed, here content is derived from a Pydantic
  [`ValidationError`][pydantic_core.ValidationError]
* a tool raised a [`ModelRetry`][pydantic_ai.exceptions.ModelRetry] exception
* no tool was found for the tool name
* the model returned plain text when a structured response was expected
* Pydantic validation of a structured response failed, here content is derived from a Pydantic
  [`ValidationError`][pydantic_core.ValidationError]
* an output validator raised a [`ModelRetry`][pydantic_ai.exceptions.ModelRetry] exception

RolloutCreate.title: RolloutCreate
RolloutCreate.description: Request body for creating a rollout.

Typed content for a component-version rollout being created/started.

Emitted once when an operator creates a rollout via the admin API. Rollouts
are platform-wide (cross-org) fleet operations, so the envelope carries no

RuntimeVaultSensitivity.description: Sensitivity classification for a Runtime Vault entry.
RuntimeVaultSensitivity.title: RuntimeVaultSensitivity

mode.title: Mode
mode.description: Per-org scanner mode override.  None → fall back to the service's global SCANNER_MODE env var (default: redact).  redact splices matched secrets out of the outbound body in flight and forwards the request; strict does the same but fails-closed (503) on scanner infrastructure errors; log_only audits matches without mutating or blocking the request (opt-in — the secret reaches upstream).

ScannerSettings.title: ScannerSettings
ScannerSettings.description:
Typed exfiltration-scanner settings for an org.

Write access is restricted to org owners.  Enterprise tenants need
to control scanner enforcement themselves.  The corresponding attack
vector — a compromised owner credential weakening enforcement — is
mitigated at the access layer (MFA, audit logs on the org_settings
table) rather than by hiding the control entirely.

Three modes (``log_only`` retained as an opt-in safety valve
alongside the redaction modes — see migration ``scanredact01`` and #256):

- ``redact`` (default) — on a match, the matched secret occurrences
  are spliced out of the **outbound request body in flight** and
  replaced with a ``[redacted:<key_path>]`` placeholder, then the
  request is forwarded.  The secret never reaches the upstream
  provider, and the conversation keeps working (no "poison-pill"
  where a leaked value re-trips a hard block on every subsequent
  turn).  A secret detected in an anomalous vector (custom header,
  URL path/query) is still **blocked** — there is no benign
  redact-and-continue story for that.  Scanner infrastructure errors
  fail **open** (request allowed) so a scanner outage can't take down
  legitimate traffic.
- ``strict`` — identical match handling (redact body, block anomalous
  vectors), but scanner infrastructure errors fail **closed** (503).
  For high-assurance workloads that prefer to fail a request rather
  than forward it unscanned.
- ``log_only`` — a match is **audited but not acted on**: the body is
  not mutated and the request is forwarded unchanged.  This is the
  safety valve for false positives (redaction mutates the payload, so
  a benign string colliding with a secret value would otherwise garble
  a legitimate request) and for rollout / investigation.  It is the
  one mode that lets the secret reach upstream, so it is strictly
  opt-in; the service default stays ``redact``.

SecretExecProviderConfig.title: SecretExecProviderConfig
SecretExecProviderConfig.description: OpenClaw exec secret provider configuration for Botyard Runtime Vault leases.

SecretExfiltrationBlockedContent.title: SecretExfiltrationBlockedContent
SecretExfiltrationBlockedContent.description: Typed content for a scanner match that blocked the outbound request.

SecretExfiltrationDetectedContent.title: SecretExfiltrationDetectedContent
SecretExfiltrationDetectedContent.description:
Typed content for a scanner match that was detected and allowed through.

Retained for backward compatibility with historical audit rows written
before forward-only redaction; new matches emit
``SecretExfiltrationRedactedContent`` instead.

Deliberately metadata-only: no raw payload, no secret value, and no secret
key path. The envelope resource_id carries the matched key path so existing
audit filters can find affected secrets without duplicating it in content.

SecretExfiltrationRedactedContent.title: SecretExfiltrationRedactedContent
SecretExfiltrationRedactedContent.description:
Typed content for a scanner match that was redacted in flight.

Emitted in ``redact``/``strict`` mode when one or more secret
occurrences were spliced out of the outbound request body before
forwarding upstream, so the request proceeds without the secret
rather than blocking the conversation.

Like the other exfiltration content types this is metadata-only: no
raw payload, no secret value, no key path in content (the envelope
``resource_id`` carries the key path).  ``occurrences`` records how
many spans were redacted so ops can see the magnitude of a leak
without storing the leaked bytes.

TurnStatus.title: TurnStatus
TurnStatus.description:
Lifecycle state of a user message's turn within the steer/queue engine.

Persisted on the user ``conversation_messages`` row so the queue/steer
lifecycle survives a reload (it is otherwise derived live from Centrifugo
events on the client). ``None`` (the column default) means an ordinary turn
that was sent and answered without ever queueing or steering — the common
case — so the column stays sparse.

``QUEUED`` is strictly transient: every queued turn has its own request and
publish loop, which always reaches a terminal frame, so the row is never
left ``QUEUED`` once that loop ends. The stored DB value is the member name
(e.g. ``QUEUED``); the lowercase value is what serializes to clients.

``DEQUEUED`` is named for the ``chat.dequeued`` frame that triggers it — a
turn that *was* queued and has since been released to run. It is distinct
from the frontend's transient ``userTurnStatus="picked_up"`` (the universal
"bot is actively processing this turn" state, re-derived live from the
active-turn lease) — that state is never persisted here.

``CANCELLED`` is terminal: the user cancelled the turn while it was still
``QUEUED`` (before it ran), so the bot dropped it from its ordering gate and
it produced no answer of its own. Distinct from ``FAILED`` (which is an
error) — a cancel is a deliberate, non-error dismissal.

This allows referencing files that have been uploaded via provider-specific file APIs

- [`XaiModel`][pydantic_ai.models.xai.XaiModel]

media_type.title: Media Type
media_type.description:
Return the media type of the file, inferred from `file_id` if not explicitly provided.

Note: Inference relies on the file extension in `file_id`.
For opaque file IDs (e.g., `'file-abc123'`), the media type will default to `'application/octet-stream'`.
Inference relies on Python's `mimetypes` module, whose results may vary across platforms.

Required by some providers (e.g., Bedrock) for certain file types.

identifier.title: Identifier
identifier.description:
The identifier of the file, such as a unique ID.

This identifier can be provided to the model in a message to allow it to refer to this file in a tool call argument,
and the tool can look up the file in question by iterating over the message history and finding the matching `UploadedFile`.

This identifier is only automatically passed to the model when the `UploadedFile` is returned by a tool.
If you're passing the `UploadedFile` as a user message, it's up to you to include a separate text part with the identifier,
e.g. "This is file <identifier>:" preceding the `UploadedFile`.

VaultInputRequestState.title: VaultInputRequestState
VaultInputRequestState.description:
Live state of a request group, returned by the serving endpoint.

The card polls/fetches this by ``request_id`` (the group id) to decide whether
to show the editable form (``pending``) or a consumed confirmation
(``submitted``), without ever exposing any value. The group shares one
lifecycle status; ``key_paths`` lists every key the secrets were (or will be)
written to.

VaultInputRequestStatus.description:
Lifecycle status of a bot-initiated Runtime Vault input request.

``PENDING`` accepts exactly one submission, which transitions it to
``SUBMITTED`` (one-shot). ``CANCELLED`` is reserved for a future explicit
close/expiry path.
VaultInputRequestStatus.title: VaultInputRequestStatus

secrets.title: Secrets
secrets.description: One entry per secret in the request group; must cover every secret in the group and no others.

allow_all_bots.title: Allow All Bots
allow_all_bots.description: When true, every bot in the org may lease these secrets. When false, access is limited to the requesting bot plus any bots in ``bot_ids``. Requires org-owner role (cross-bot distribution is owner-gated).

bot_ids.title: Bot Ids
bot_ids.description: Additional bots to grant access to. Ignored when allow_all_bots is true. The requesting bot is always granted access regardless. Granting extra bots requires org-owner role.

VaultInputSubmitRequest.title: VaultInputSubmitRequest
VaultInputSubmitRequest.description:
Body for submitting a bot-requested batch of Runtime Vault secret values.

Carries one ``VaultSecretSubmission`` per secret in the request group — and it
must be **all of them** (submission is all-or-nothing: every secret is stored
in one transaction, or none is). Bot scoping (``allow_all_bots``/``bot_ids``)
applies to the whole group and is owner-gated, the same choice as the normal
"create secret" modal, just without setting the values blind.

request_id.title: Request Id
request_id.description: Id of this secret's row within the request group (from the card).

value.title: Value
value.description: The secret value to store in the Runtime Vault.

key_path.title: Key Path
key_path.description: Optional edited full Vault key path (validated as a SecretKeyPath). Omit to keep the key the bot requested. Whether an existing key may be updated is fixed by the bot's original request, not this body: a committed secret at the resolved key is rejected unless the bot's pending request opted into an update AND the requesting bot is authorized for it.

display_name.title: Display Name
display_name.description: Optional override for the secret's display name.

description.title: Description
description.description: Optional override for the secret's description.

max_ttl_seconds.title: Max Ttl Seconds
max_ttl_seconds.description: Optional override for the maximum lease TTL.

VaultSecretSubmission.title: VaultSecretSubmission
VaultSecretSubmission.description:
One secret's user-supplied value (plus any edited metadata) within a submit.

``request_id`` identifies which secret of the request group this value is for
(the id the card carried on each field). ``value`` is required; the rest let
the user tweak the bot-suggested entry metadata before it is written. Omitted
fields fall back to what the bot originally requested. ``value`` is a
``SecretStr`` so it is never rendered in logs or error reprs.

identifier.title: Identifier
identifier.description:
The identifier of the file, such as a unique ID.

This identifier can be provided to the model in a message to allow it to refer to this file in a tool call argument,
and the tool can look up the file in question by iterating over the message history and finding the matching `FileUrl`.

This identifier is only automatically passed to the model when the `FileUrl` is returned by a tool.
If you're passing the `FileUrl` as a user message, it's up to you to include a separate text part with the identifier,
e.g. "This is file <identifier>:" preceding the `FileUrl`.

It's also included in inline-text delimiters for providers that require inlining text documents, so the model can
distinguish multiple files.

WorkforceTaskLinkDirection.title: WorkforceTaskLinkDirection
WorkforceTaskLinkDirection.description:
A task link as seen from one task's perspective.

Not stored: computed when projecting a task's links. ``OUTGOING`` means the
viewing task is the ``from`` side of the link row; ``INCOMING`` means it is
the ``to`` side. Combined with the link type this yields the display label —
e.g. ``BLOCKS`` + ``INCOMING`` renders as "blocked by". On writes it lets a
task express an incoming link (e.g. "this task is blocked by X") without a
redundant inverse enum member.

WorkforceTaskLinkType.title: WorkforceTaskLinkType
WorkforceTaskLinkType.description:
Canonical type of a directed link between two workforce tasks.

Stored on ``workforce_task_links.link_type``. ``RELATES_TO`` is symmetric
(order-independent — a pair is stored once, in a canonical task-id order);
``BLOCKS`` is directional — the ``from`` task blocks the ``to`` task.

The reverse "blocked by" relationship is a *derived view* of a ``BLOCKS``
row seen from the ``to`` task's side (see ``WorkforceTaskLinkDirection``),
never a stored value — one relationship is always exactly one row.

AssistantAbortedEvent.description:
The in-flight assistant turn was aborted by a /stop (or one of the
other abort triggers — bare ``stop``, ``abort``, ``cancel``, etc.).

Non-terminal — a follow-up ``assistant.error`` (or ``assistant.end``)
closes the turn out for real. The dedicated event exists so clients
can distinguish "stopped on purpose" from "network failure / timeout"
and render a "Stopped" badge instead of an error toast.

``request_id`` identifies the message being aborted (NOT the /stop
message that triggered the abort). The /stop message is its own
chat.request with its own request_id.

conversation_id.description: ID of the conversation channel this event was published to. Lets a client verify a realtime frame belongs to the channel it subscribed for and drop cross-channel misdeliveries. Optional so the field can roll out ahead of every publisher and consumer without a lockstep deploy; real publishers always set it.
conversation_id.title: Conversation Id

aborted_at.description: Unix ms timestamp of the abort
aborted_at.title: Aborted At

aborted_by.description: Actor id (sender_id) that issued the /stop, when known.
aborted_by.title: Aborted By

AssistantCancelledEvent.description:
A still-queued request was cancelled by the user before it ever ran.

Terminal — the bot pulled it out of the ordering gate and dropped it (it
never dispatched, so there is no visible assistant response of its own). The
dedicated event lets connected clients drop the message from the queued tray
and render a "Cancelled" marker immediately, without a refetch. The durable
truth is the persisted ``turn_status='cancelled'``.

``request_id`` identifies the cancelled queued message.

starting at 0 — lets the client detect gaps within a single turn,
distinct from Centrifugo's per-channel offset.

AssistantEndEvent.description:

``message_id`` and ``ordinal`` identify the cold-started answer row (claimed
at ``chat.picked_up``, before any content). Carrying both here lets a client

immediately — without waiting on the follow-up ``message.new`` or a refetch

ContentBlockPayloadRef.description:
Reference to the conversation-scoped file holding a block's heavy data.

The bytes live in conversation-scoped storage owned end-to-end by
``ContentBlockService`` and are served at read time by the content-block
serving endpoint, exactly like conversation attachments — so access is
gated by access to the conversation. The block persists an opaque, durable
``payload_id`` (never a storage key or presigned URL): the service *derives*
the storage location from the caller's already-authorized
``(org, conversation, payload_id)`` context, so clients never see — and can
never supply — a raw object key. The message read path's re-signers only
refresh user-prompt parts and the ``media`` column, not tool-return
metadata, so an ID that is resolved server-side on every read is also what
keeps references from going stale.

payload_id.description: Opaque durable ID of the payload document (a UUID). Hand it back to the content-block serving endpoint / read tool together with the conversation to fetch the rows — storage location is server-internal.
payload_id.title: Payload Id

conversation_id.description: Conversation the payload file is scoped to; gates read access.
conversation_id.title: Conversation Id

content_type.description: MIME type of the payload file (``application/json`` for chart/table data).
content_type.title: Content Type

byte_size.description: Payload size in bytes — lets the frontend show a size hint / guard huge fetches.
byte_size.title: Byte Size

ContentBlockPayloadRef.title: ContentBlockPayloadRef

ContentBlockResult.description:
What ``emit_content_block`` returns.

``block`` is the slim reference the frontend renders (carried on the tool
return's structured output). ``summary`` is the only part the model reads
back — a one-line description so the agent can refer to what it emitted
without re-ingesting the payload.

block.description: The content block the frontend renders.
block.title: Block

summary.description: One-line summary for the model (the only part it re-reads).
summary.title: Summary

ContentBlockResult.title: ContentBlockResult

DiagramContentBlock.description:
A rendered diagram (Mermaid). The diagram source lives in the payload file.

The inline block carries only tiny frame metadata (title/description/height);
the potentially-large Mermaid source text is the heavy payload, stored in the
referenced file exactly like a chart's rows — so the diagram source never
crosses the chat/LLM boundary. The frontend fetches the source by
``payload_id`` and renders it with Mermaid (lazy-loaded, console-side); the
widget/embed bundle, which excludes the heavy renderer, falls back to showing
the source text.

``height`` is an optional max render height in px; the emit tool clamps a
supplied value into ``[DIAGRAM_HEIGHT_MIN_PX, DIAGRAM_HEIGHT_MAX_PX]`` so a
stored block can't request an absurd card size. ``None`` means auto-size.

title.description: Optional title shown above the diagram.
title.title: Title

height.description: Optional max render height in px (auto-size when omitted). Clamped by the emit tool to a sane bounded range.
height.title: Height

payload.description: Conversation file holding the Mermaid source text.

DiagramContentBlock.title: DiagramContentBlock

TableContentBlock.description:
A data table. Rows live in the referenced payload file.

Column headers are inline (small, needed to render the header + column
order); the ``rows`` array (``list[list[str | int | float | bool | None]]``)
is stored in ``payload``.

payload.description: Conversation file holding the row data.

TableContentBlock.title: TableContentBlock

TaskContentBlock.description:
A snapshot of a workforce board task, rendered as a first-class card.

Unlike ``chart`` / ``table`` / ``diagram`` (whose heavy data lives in a
referenced payload file), a task is bounded and small, so its fields are
captured **inline** at emit time — no payload file, no serving round-trip.
The snapshot is server-resolved from the real task (the bot only references
it by ``workforce_slug`` + ``task_number``), so the values are trustworthy
rather than bot-supplied. Fields freeze at emit time; live auto-refresh is a
deliberate follow-up.

``org_slug`` + ``workforce_slug`` + ``task_number`` drive the "Open in board"
deeplink to the live task.

org_slug.description: Org slug of the task's board, for the board deeplink.
org_slug.title: Org Slug

workforce_slug.description: Slug of the workforce board the task lives on.
workforce_slug.title: Workforce Slug

workforce_name.description: Display name of the workforce board (header crumb).
workforce_name.title: Workforce Name

task_number.description: The task's human-friendly number within the workforce.
task_number.title: Task Number

status.description: Display name of the task's current column (e.g. 'In Review').
status.title: Status

description_preview.description: Short, truncated preview of the task description (may be null).
description_preview.title: Description Preview

assignee_kind.description: Whether a bot or a human is assigned, or null when unassigned.
assignee_kind.title: Assignee Kind

github_pr_url.description: Linked GitHub PR URL, or null.
github_pr_url.title: Github Pr Url

linked_conversation_count.description: Number of conversations linked to the task.
linked_conversation_count.title: Linked Conversation Count

TaskContentBlock.title: TaskContentBlock

VaultInputRequest.description:
A bot-initiated request for the user to supply a Runtime Vault secret value.

Rendered as an interactive form card. The ``reason`` is shown prominently so
the user can judge *why* their own bot is asking for a secret before typing
one (the consent surface). The value is entered into a masked field and
written directly to the Vault — never into this block, the transcript, or any
realtime event.

request_id.description: Durable group id of the backing ``vault_input_requests`` rows (one per secret, sharing this id). The card fetches live status by this id and submits the whole group against it.
request_id.title: Request Id

reason.description: Why the bot needs these secret(s) — shown prominently to the user as the consent prompt for the whole request. Should name the purpose, not free-form 'paste anything'.
reason.title: Reason

secrets.description: The pre-filled Vault entries to complete (one or more).
secrets.title: Secrets

status.description: Status at emit time (always ``pending``). The live status is fetched from the serving endpoint by ``request_id``; the transcript copy is immutable.

VaultInputRequest.title: VaultInputRequest

VaultInputRequestResult.description:
What ``request_vault_input`` returns.

``block`` is the slim reference the frontend renders (carried on the tool
return's structured output). ``summary`` is the only part the model reads
back — a one-line description so the agent can refer to what it asked for
without re-deriving it.

block.description: The input-request card the frontend renders.

summary.description: One-line summary for the model (the only part it re-reads).
summary.title: Summary

VaultInputRequestResult.title: VaultInputRequestResult

VaultSecretField.description:
One pre-filled Runtime Vault entry the user is asked to complete.

Carries everything ``create_secret`` needs **except** the value: the full
``key_path`` (bot-suggested, user-editable) and the metadata the bot suggests.
The value is supplied by the user at submit time and written straight to the
Vault. ``request_id`` is this secret's stable id within the request group — the
card echoes it back on submit so each entered value is matched to the right row.

request_id.description: Stable id of this secret's backing row within the request group. The card submits each value tagged with this id.
request_id.title: Request Id

key_path.description: The Runtime Vault key path the secret will be written to (e.g. ``garmin.password``). Bot-suggested; the user may edit it before submitting.
key_path.title: Key Path

description.description: Optional description of what the secret is for (bot-suggested).
description.title: Description

max_ttl_seconds.description: Maximum lease TTL bots may later request for this secret.
max_ttl_seconds.title: Max Ttl Seconds

sensitivity.description: How the stored value is classified: ``secret`` (masked in the UI and checked by the exfiltration scanner) or ``plain`` (a non-sensitive config value). Bot-fixed at request time — the card surfaces it read-only so the user can see how their value will be treated, and it is not sent back on submit; the submit path resolves it from the pending record.

allow_update.description: Whether this secret targets an *existing* Vault key whose value the bot asked to replace, rather than a brand-new key. The card surfaces this only to render the 'replaces an existing secret' badge — it is not sent back on submit; the bot's intent is bound to the pending record and the submit path resolves it from there, writing to an existing key only when the requesting bot is already authorized for it.
allow_update.title: Allow Update

secret_ttl_seconds.description: Optional whole-secret lifetime: the stored secret auto-expires this many seconds after it is submitted, then is lazily deleted. None means it never expires. Shown to the user as part of the consent prompt.
secret_ttl_seconds.title: Secret Ttl Seconds

VaultSecretField.title: VaultSecretField
